items.content_statement: Democracy is a system in which citizens choose leaders through elections
items.classification_rationale: Definitional propositional knowledge - factual content about what democracy is

items.content_statement: Democracy can be contrasted with other forms of government, such as monarchy and dictatorship
items.classification_rationale: Conceptual understanding of how democracy relates to other government systems - transferable conceptual knowledge

items.content_statement: Value of democracy in ensuring fairness, equality, citizen voice, and peaceful leadership change
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how democracy functions and its benefits - a conceptual claim about how political systems work that learners must understand

items.content_statement: There are three-yearly general elections for the House of Representatives in New Zealand.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about electoral timing - hard prerequisite sequence applies as this is foundational civic knowledge

items.content_statement: House of Representatives as law-making body of New Zealand
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about institutional role - factual content about how government works

items.content_statement: Government formation by the party or coalition holding majority support in Parliament.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how democratic government formation works - factual content about parliamentary systems

items.content_statement: The Prime Minister is the head of government, leading the Cabinet of ministers.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about governmental structure - factual content that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: The Government is the central team making decisions on core government responsibilities such as schools, hospitals, roads, and public safety and deciding public spending.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how government functions - factual content about governmental structure and responsibilities

items.content_statement: Local councils are elected by the community to make our towns and cities better places to live
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how local government works - factual content about democratic processes

items.content_statement: Local councils look after things like parks and playgrounds, libraries and swimming pools, rubbish and recycling, roads and footpaths, planning where buildings like houses can be built
items.classification_rationale: Factual knowledge about the specific responsibilities and services provided by local councils

items.content_statement: Impact of Ancient Greek political ideas on New Zealand's democracy
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability across multiple lenses - requires reasoning about historical influence, political concepts, and contemporary democratic systems through multiple disciplinary frameworks

items.content_statement: Voting for leaders: first election in 1853 in which people chose leaders, like in Ancient Greece.
items.classification_rationale: Historical factual content about specific dates and events - propositional knowledge that must be understood

items.content_statement: Parliament, in which groups met to make laws, began in 1854
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information with specific date - propositional knowledge

items.content_statement: Māori and European settlers talked before the Constitution was written and had meetings to decide how New Zealand should be run
items.classification_rationale: Factual historical information about constitutional process - propositional knowledge

items.content_statement: Parliamentary democracy involves groups meeting to make laws together, similar to Greek councils
items.classification_rationale: Conceptual understanding of democratic governance structure - transferable concept

items.content_statement: Democratic principles of ensuring fairness, equality, and citizen voice
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what democratic principles are and how they function - conceptual content that can be understood and explained

items.content_statement: Civic participation and contribution can be increased through: volunteering at school or in the community joining youth councils or school leadership groups attending hui or public meetings giving feedback in community consultations taking part in local projects or fundraising for good causes
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about methods for increasing civic participation - factual content about how civic engagement works

items.content_statement: Governments develop laws for a range of purposes, such as to prevent harm, protect wellbeing, and maintain order
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the purposes of laws - factual content about how governments function

items.content_statement: Laws are legally binding rules that everyone in the country must follow
items.classification_rationale: Definitional knowledge about what laws are - factual content defining the nature and scope of laws

items.content_statement: National laws, such as traffic rules (e.g. driving on the left, seat-belt requirements)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about specific laws and regulations - factual content that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Local bylaws, such as noise limits in residential areas or pet control requirements (e.g. leashes, dog-exercise areas)
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what local bylaws are and examples of their scope - factual content to be known

items.content_statement: Our community rules for safety and fairness
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what community rules exist and their purpose - factual content that can be assessed through knowledge check

items.content_statement: Everyone is treated equally under the law.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about how the legal system works - factual content about equal treatment principle

items.content_statement: Protection of individuals and property
items.classification_rationale: Propositional content about a fundamental purpose/function of laws and judicial systems - conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation or application

items.content_statement: Courts help make sure laws are followed and fair decisions are made based on those laws.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the function of courts in the legal system - factual content about how institutions work

items.content_statement: In cases where someone is found guilty of breaking the law, courts can give punishments such as fines, community service, or time in prison.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how the judicial system works - factual content about court procedures and sentencing options

items.content_statement: Courts also help solve disagreements between people and protect individual rights.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the functions of courts - factual content about how the judicial system works

items.content_statement: Part of the role of the courts is to ensure justice is done.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about how the judicial system works - describes a factual relationship between courts and their function that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Judges are neutral decision-makers, interpreting laws and ensuring fair outcomes.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the role and function of judges in the judicial system - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Law enforcement, crime investigation, and crime prevention
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about three distinct areas of the legal system - factual content about what these domains involve

items.content_statement: Emergency response and personal safety via 111
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about emergency services access - factual content about how to access emergency response

items.content_statement: School Community Officers support safe learning environments
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the role/function of School Community Officers - factual content about how the world works

items.content_statement: Legal foundations of rights in New Zealand
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about legal foundations - factual content that must be understood

items.content_statement: New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990: Protects rights such as freedom of speech, belief, and peaceful protest.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what the Act protects - factual content that can be known and recalled

items.content_statement: Human Rights Act 1993: Protects people from unfair discrimination.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what a specific law does - factual content that can be known and assessed through knowledge check

items.content_statement: Electoral Act 1993: Defines voting rights and how elections work.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about what the Electoral Act 1993 defines - factual content about legal framework

items.content_statement: Te Tiriti o Waitangi | the Treaty of Waitangi are foundational documents that inform the rights and responsibilities of individuals and institutions in New Zealand
items.classification_rationale: This is propositional knowledge about the foundational role of Te Tiriti/Treaty - a conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation of how these documents inform rights and responsibilities

items.content_statement: Tangata whenua and tangata Tiriti are partners in a bicultural society.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about how New Zealand society is structured - a conceptual understanding about the nature of the relationship between Māori and non-Māori in New Zealand

items.content_statement: International agreements (e.g. Universal Declaration of Human Rights) shape New Zealand's commitment to global human rights.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how international agreements influence national commitments - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Obeying New Zealand's laws to help keep society fair and safe
items.classification_rationale: Describes a sustained default orientation - law-abiding behaviour that operates across contexts as an enacted capacity over time

items.content_statement: Enrolling to vote when eligible
items.classification_rationale: This describes a sustained civic orientation - the disposition to participate in democratic processes by default when opportunities arise, rather than an occasion-triggered skill deployed only when prompted

items.content_statement: Paying taxes to support services like hospitals, schools, and transport
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about civic responsibility and how tax systems fund public services - factual content about how society functions

items.content_statement: Respecting the rights and freedoms of others
items.classification_rationale: This describes a sustained default orientation - a quality that exists only as it is enacted over time across contexts, not an occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: Acting in ways that support the wellbeing of the community and country
items.classification_rationale: Describes a sustained default orientation - a way of acting that operates across contexts by default, not an occasion-triggered skill deployed when situations call for it

items.content_statement: Using trustworthy sources, understanding different viewpoints, and thinking critically about news and information
items.classification_rationale: Analytical capability deployed when encountering news and information - occasion-triggered skill requiring reasoning quality assessment

items.content_statement: Responsibilities help people live together peacefully. They build trust and cooperation between people.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claims about how responsibilities function in social contexts

items.content_statement: They protect everyone's rights. When people do their part, everyone benefits.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claim about how rights protection and civic participation create mutual benefits

items.content_statement: Without shared responsibility, society can become unfair or unsafe.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about how the world works - causal relationship between shared responsibility and societal outcomes

items.content_statement: Having rights also means doing your part to help others enjoy theirs
items.classification_rationale: This is a propositional claim about how rights work - a conceptual relationship between individual rights and reciprocal responsibilities that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Informed citizens are needed to participate in democracy
items.classification_rationale: Propositional claim about how democracy works - a conceptual relationship between citizen knowledge and democratic participation that learners must understand

halted_blocks.source_block_raw_text: The facts, concepts, principles, and theories to teach.
halted_blocks.diagnostic: meta_or_nonteachable - this is a definitional statement about curriculum categories, not teachable content with observable learning outcomes

halted_blocks.source_block_raw_text: The skills, strategies, and applications to teach.

halted_blocks.diagnostic: meta_or_nonteachable - this is a standalone section heading with no teachable content, propositional claim, or behavioral expectation

halted_blocks.source_block_raw_text: Basis for a community with reliable safeguards, fostering orderly and peaceful functioning.
halted_blocks.diagnostic: The content is purely aspirational with no teachable destination - it describes abstract qualities ('reliable safeguards', 'orderly and peaceful functioning') without any propositional claims about how laws work, analytical skills for evaluating legal systems, or observable behaviors that could be assessed.
meta_or_nonteachable - this is a section header/navigation label with no teachable content or substantive claim

The phrase 'Traffic control and road-safety education' appears to be a topic label or heading with no teachable claim, learning expectation, or behavioural referent. It names a subject area but contains no propositional content to understand, no skill to deploy, and no orientation to sustain.

This is a section heading or navigation label with no substantive teachable content - just the phrase 'Core responsibilities of citizenship' without any specification of what those responsibilities are or what learners should know, understand, or be able to do regarding them.

This is a section heading or navigation label with no teachable content - just the phrase 'Importance of shared responsibility' without any substantive claim, skill description, or behavioral expectation that could be operationalized

halted_blocks.source_block_raw_text: how information can be collected about the ways people in the school community and local community volunteer their time how to engage in dialogue about the core ideas of democracy, fairness, and equality in relation to a current or local issue how to participate in a full experience of a voting process how information can be collected about laws and by-laws that need to be followed on the journey to and from school and considering why those laws are in place and how they might need to change the role of police in keeping people safe, investigating crime, and responding to emergencies how to survey and seek a range of viewpoints on a current or proposed law what makes for a good rule and a good law and considering why people may have different perspectives examples of rights and responsibilities and how these apply to them in everyday life engaging in dialogue about why rights and responsibilities matter for keeping society fair, safe, and cooperative how the significance of a key law that protects rights in New Zealand (e.g. Human Rights Act) can be assessed using a case study how international agreements (e.g. UN Declaration of Human Rights) influence New Zealand’s approach to human rights the responsibilities of citizenship, including obeying laws, voting, paying taxes, and respecting others then develop a campaign and associated materials for raising awareness of the rights and responsibilities of citizens in New Zealand
